figures.caption: Figure 2: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model. In the heatmap, the five highest-scoring heads are highlighted with green rectangles. In the line chart, the head role is aligned with the heat map; each layer score is computed by averaging the AIE scores of the top 15% highest-scoring heads in that layer; the red dashed line shows the layer most responsible for rule selection.
Figure 3: Circuit network on top-5 attention head scores associated with reasoning components.
Figure 6: Distribution of uncertain token (low token probability) grouped by reasoning component on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Qwen3-8B.
Figure 7: Distribution of uncertain token (low token probability) grouped by reasoning component on phi-4 and Qwen3-4B.
Figure 12: Circuit network on top-5 attention head scores associated with reasoning components in the Qwen3-8B model.
Figure 13: Circuit network on top-5 attention head scores associated with reasoning components in the Qwen3-4B model.

Figure 1: Overview of this framework. Given a deductive reasoning problem description (xq) together with a few-shot prompt containing demonstrations [(x1, y1), (x2, y2), xq] (left side of the figure), the LLM is expected to generate a Chain-of-Thought reasoning process that follows the demonstrated template. The deductive reasoning problem can be represented as an inference graph composed of multiple inference steps, each of which can be further decomposed into salient reasoning components (e.g., premise selection) that are indicated by low-probability tokens across diverse LLMs (bottom graph in this figure). Subsequently, Causal Mediation Analysis techniques are employed to investigate the reasoning circuits of LLMs.
Figure 5: Effect of knocking out top-k logical reasoning heads on MMLU and ProntoQA datasets.
Figure 11: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on phi-4 model..
Figure 10: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on Qwen3-4B model.

Figure 8: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model. In the heatmap, the five highest-scoring heads are highlighted with green rectangles. The head roles in the line chart are aligned with the second row heat map; each layer score is computed by averaging the AIE scores of the top 15% highest-scoring heads in that layer; the red dashed line show the layer most responsible for rule selection.
Figure 9: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on Qwen3-8B model.
Table 5: Example of a corrupt prompt type: modifying rule content affects the rule selection component.
Table 6: Example of a corrupt prompt type: changing the traversal algorithm in demonstrations affects the premise selection component.
Table 4: Example of a corrupt prompt type: modifying rule content affects premise selection termination.

Table 3: Example of a corrupt prompt type: modifying the facts affects premise selection. The blue text represents the content of the causal span (as well as the corrupted positions) in the clean prompt, while the red text represents the corrupted content, and the gray text will be ignored. The last corrupted position corresponds to the reasoning component, where we inspect the logit difference under changes in the causal spans (causal positions).
Table 2: Procedure to synthesize the pair of clean and corrupted prompts. The corrupted position is the causal elements that affect the reasoning component of interest.

Figure 15: Inference step accuracy when knockout top-k logical reasoning heads on synthesized data (Dsyn) and on Logical Reasoning task, ProofWriter dataset (k = 8 with phi-4 model and k = 5 with other models).
Figure 14: Circuit network on top-5 attention head scores associated with reasoning components in the phi-4 model.
